journal-title-group.journal-title: Journal of Human Nutrition and Dietetics

title-group.article-title: Dietary n‐3 Polyunsaturated Fatty Acids From Fish Are Associated With Better Healthy Aging Indicators: Results of the DIAPELH Study

aff.named-content: Department of Nutritional Science and Dietetics

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2025 The Author(s). Journal of Human Nutrition and Dietetics published by John Wiley & Sons Ltd on behalf of British Dietetic Association.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the terms of the http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/ License, which permits use and distribution in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited, the use is non‐commercial and no modifications or adaptations are made.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Promoting healthy aging is a public health goal, especially in regions with a high proportion of older adults, such as Greece. This cross‐sectional study investigated the association of fish n‐3 PUFA intake with indicators of healthy aging among older Greek Peloponnesian adults.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: In total, 449 individuals > 60 years of age were enroled. Sociodemographic, anthropometrical, medical, mobility, balance, lifestyle, dietary, cognitive and mental characteristics were assessed through validated questionnaires and procedures.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Analyses revealed that n‐3 PUFA intake was associated with fewer depression symptoms (p < 0.001), higher cognition levels (p = 0.012) and levels of healthy aging (p < 0.001), derived from Successful Aging Index (SAI). In parallel, n‐3 PUFA intake was associated with higher adherence to the Mediterranean diet (p < 0.001). Additionally, n‐3 PUFA intake was inversely correlated with the presence of long COVID‐19 symptomatology (p = 0.036). No association of n‐3 PUFA intake with mobility or physical performance and balance (all ps> 0.05) was detected.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: The results underscore the significance of nutrition in older adults, highlighting the possible protective impact of n‐3 PUFAs on maintaining functionality. Future prospective studies may validate these associations and contribute to the development of targeted nutritional strategies for older adults.

list-item.p: For each 1 g increase in n‐3 PUFA intake by older adults living in Peloponnese (Greece), an approximately 0.5/10‐unit increase in SAI score, a 0.7/30‐unit increase in MoCA and a 0.67/15‐unit decrease in GDS score were observed.
Memory, Attention and Executive MoCa domains were positively associated with n‐3 PUFA intake among older adults in Peloponnese.
Higher intake of n‐3 PUFA (per mg/day) was significantly associated with lower odds of long COVID symptoms.

institution-wrap.institution: The authors received no specific funding for this work.

p.mixed-citation: A. Foscolou , T. Pratti , P. Detopoulou , et al., “Dietary n‐3 Polyunsaturated Fatty Acids From Fish Are Associated With Better Healthy Aging Indicators: Results of the DIAPELH Study,” Journal of Human Nutrition and Dietetics 38 (2025): 1‐9, 10.1111/jhn.70169.PMC1264123641277355

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: By 2050, the global population of older people (i.e., over 60 years of age) is expected to increase significantly due to population aging and increasing life expectancy [1]. More specifically, in 2023, the number of older adults was approximately 1.1 billion, while by 2030 this number is expected to be 1.4 billion, and by 2050, it will reach up to 2.1 billion [2]. As life expectancy increases, the challenge is not only to survive at older ages but to maintain quality of life. Physical and mental functioning are critical factors for autonomy, social participation and reduced burden of the health care system [3]. The ultimate goal is to achieve healthy aging, defined by the World Health Organization as ‘the process of developing and maintaining the functional ability that enables wellbeing in older age’ [4] or as otherwise proposed by Rowe and Kahn [5], a multifaceted construct that encompasses, among other components, the absence of disease [5]. The aging process, most probably, cannot be reversed; however, it can be prolonged healthily following a healthy lifestyle and healthy dietary patterns [6].

Diet has a significant impact on overall health, the aging process and, consequently, healthy aging [7]. However, a considerable proportion of older adults fail to meet their nutritional requirements [8]. For example, research indicates that many older adults frequently fail to consume enough n‐3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (n‐3 PUFAs), among other nutrients [9]. N‐3 PUFAs are naturally present in certain foods (i.e., fish, nuts, seeds and plant oils) and are also added to certain fortified foods (e.g., dairy, eggs, baked products). Sufficient fish consumption constitutes a primary source of bioavailable n‐3 PUFAs [10]. PUFAs, along with monounsaturated fatty acids, appear to help slow the aging process, as seen by an increase in telomere length [11]. N‐3 PUFAs, particularly eicosapentaenoic acid (EPA) and docosahexaenoic acid (DHA), found mostly on fish n‐3 PUFAs, have been well documented for their beneficial effects on cardiovascular health [12], regulating inflammation [13] and maintaining cognitive [14] and mental function [15]. Studies have also linked n‐3 PUFA intake to better muscle performance, including gait speed and time up and test [16], common measurements of muscle health and performance for older adults and overall a critical factor for maintaining autonomy in old age. At the same time, n‐3 PUFA and other microconstituents of the Mediterranean diet exert a protective effect against COVID‐19 [17] and long COVID‐19 [18].

In this context, adequate intake of n‐3 PUFA may be protective in multiple areas related to the functionality and quality of life of older adults. However, there are limited data on the association of dietary intake of n‐3 PUFA, especially through fish consumption, on multidimensional indicators of healthy aging in Mediterranean populations. Thus, the aim of the present work was to investigate the association of dietary n‐3 fatty acids intake from fish consumption with indicators of healthy aging, such as mental and cognitive health, mobility and balance, the presence of long COVID‐19 symptomatology, as well as the level of successful aging of older Greek Peloponnesian adults and their adherence to the Mediterranean diet.

sec.title: Study Design and Participants
sec.p: The present cross‐sectional study is part of the DIAPELH project (derived from the Greek acronym ‘ΔΙΑ.ΠΕΛ.Η – ΔΙΑτροφή στην ΠΕΛοπόννησο των Ηλικιωμένων”). DIAPELH is an ongoing study in the region of Peloponnese, which aims to investigate the association between several socioeconomical, anthropometrical, lifestyle, nutritional, functional, physical and mental health‐related characteristics with healthy aging indicators of community‐dwelling older Peloponnese people (over 60 years of age). A well‐experienced multidisciplinary team of health scientists (dietitians and nurses) in the field investigation collected all the required information using standard procedures. Individuals were invited to participate through written announcements (posters) in local newspapers, community centres, local shops and healthcare facilities. Recruitment was also facilitated by collaboration with municipalities throughout the region of Peloponnese. Additionally, social media (Facebook) posts and electronic invitations to ‘friendship clubs’ (places where older people can seek warmth, social contact, interpersonal relationships or simply information and entertainment) of the Peloponnese were made.

Participants of both sexes, over 60 years old and permanent residents of the Peloponnese were included in the study, while individuals accommodated in residential care homes or living in the Peloponnese for less than 5 years were excluded from the study. Individuals living in residential care homes were excluded from the study, since their dietary habits do not represent their personal dietary choices.

sec.title: Bioethics
sec.p: The present study was approved by the School of Health Sciences Research Ethics Committee at the University of Peloponnese (Approval no. 04/11‐09‐2023). The study was conducted in accordance with the principles of the Declaration of Helsinki [19]. Participants were apprised of the study's objectives and methodologies, and all provided signed informed consent before enrolment. All data were anonymized before analysis, and no personal information was preserved.

sec.title: Measurements
sec.p: A quantitative questionnaire and standard procedures were followed to gather all of the necessary information. More specifically:

sec.title: Sociodemographic and Lifestyle Characteristics
sec.p: The assessed sociodemographic characteristics encompassed age (years), sex (male/female) and family status (married/unmarried/divorced/widowed). Smoking habits were also assessed; Individuals were considered ‘smokers’ if they smoked at least one cigarette or other tobacco product daily when the interview was taking place.

sec.title: Anthropometry
sec.p: Standard procedures were implemented to determine body weight and height. Specifically, the nearest 0.1 kg was used to measure body weight and the nearest 0.1 cm to measure height [20]. Body mass index (BMI), as body weight (kg)/height2 (m2), was then calculated. Waist and hip circumferences were measured using a flexible, nonelastic tape while unclothed. Waist circumference was measured at the midpoint between the lowest rib and the iliac crest using an inelastic measuring tape to the closest 0.1 cm, while hip circumference was measured between the greater trochanter and the inferior buttock level [21]. The waist‐to‐hip ratio was used to identify abdominal obesity. Waist‐to‐hip ratio ≥ 0.90 cm for men and ≥ 0.85 cm for women were considered substantially increased [21, 22].

sec.title: Dietary Intake
sec.p: To evaluate dietary habits, a semiquantitative food frequency questionnaire (FFQ) that was both validated and reproducible was implemented [23]. The consumption of a variety of food groups and beverages, such as meat and meat products, poultry, fish, milk or other dairy products, fruits, vegetables, legumes and alcohol, was evaluated on a weekly basis (never or rarely, 1–3 times/month, 1–2 times/week, daily, 3–6 times/week, daily, more than twice daily). The n‐3 PUFA intake was assessed based on the frequency of fish consumption. More specifically, the frequency of small and big fish consumption was calculated separately as times per week, while multiple 24 h food recalls (≥ 3), as recorded within 1 month from the first face‐to‐face interview via phone interviews, were used to quantify fish consumption. Based on the USDA database [10], the mean n‐3 PUFA content of the most frequently consumed small and big fish species in Greece was determined. MedDietScore was used to evaluate the level of adherence to the Mediterranean diet (theoretical range: 0–55) [24]. Higher values indicate increased adherence.

sec.title: Depression Symptomatology
sec.p: A condensed, self‐administered Geriatric Depression Scale (GDS) (translated and validated in Greek) with a total score from 0 to 15 (GDS) was utilized to evaluate depression symptoms throughout the preceding month. Elevated scores signify greater severity of depression symptomatology [25].

sec.title: Cognitive Function
sec.p: The Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA) test was used to evaluate the cognitive function of the participants. MoCA scores out of 30 points, with higher values indicating better cognitive function. Additionally, the six MoCA domains (i.e., Memory, Language, Attention, Executive, Visuospatial and Orientation) were also assessed using an established calculation methodology [26].

sec.title: Physical Function
sec.p: The risk of falling was assessed using the Laboratory for Research of the Musculoskeletal System (LRMS) questionnaire. The LRMS consisted of 11 four‐point scale questions with a theoretical range of 0–33. Higher scores indicated a higher risk of falling in the future. Walking speed was evaluated using the 4‐Metre Walk Test (4MWT) at a usual pace. A stopwatch and floor markings indicated the 4‐metre distance. Participants wore comfortable clothing and appropriate footwear. Timing began with the first movement and stopped when the foot touched the endpoint [27]. Handgrip strength was measured using Takei A5401 digital hand dynamometer (Takei Scientific Instruments Co. Ltd., Japan). Each participant was seated with the elbow flexed at 90° and the wrist in a neutral position. The handle of the dynamometer was adjusted so that the second joint of the middle finger was also at 90° flexion. Two measurements were performed, one with each hand, and the average value was recorded in kilograms with an accuracy of 0.1 kg. Before these measurements, participants were given instructions and were asked to practice and familiarize themselves with the dynamometer, without applying force.

sec.title: Clinical Characteristics
sec.p: Long COVID‐19 symptomatology was assessed using the definition adopted by Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), that is, ‘a chronic condition that occurs after SARS‐COV‐2 infection and is present for at least 3 months’ [28]. More specifically, participants were asked if they had experienced at least one of the most common long COVID‐19 symptoms (i.e., fatigue, brain fog or postexertional malaise) for at least 3 months after their infection with SARS‐COV‐2 [29].

sec.title: Healthy Aging
sec.p: To evaluate healthy aging, a previously developed and validated tool was used, that is, the SAI (Successful Aging Index) index [30]. Education (years of education), financial status (through self‐reported mean income over the previous 3 years, categorized on a four‐point scale: low [insufficient for daily expenses], medium [covering expenses with effort], good [sufficient for daily expenses] and very good [comfortably sufficient for daily expenses]), physical activity (METs), BMI, depression (GDS), social activity (family or friends) participation (times/week), number of annual excursions, cardiovascular disease risk factors (obesity, hypertension, hypercholesterolaemia, diabetes mellitus) and level of adherence to the Mediterranean diet formed the SAI index [30].

sec.title: Statistical Analysis
sec.p: Normality was tested using the Kolmogorov‐Smirnov test. Categorical values are expressed as frequencies and percentages, while continuous variables are expressed as mean and standard deviation if normally distributed, or as median and interquartile ranges if non‐normally distributed. Participants were stratified into two groups (i.e., Low n‐3 PUFA intake and High n‐3 PUFA intake) based on the median value (i.e., 6.8 g/day) of n‐3 PUFA intake of the total sample. Comparisons of continuous variables were performed using the Mann–Whitney U test for skewed distributions and independent samples t‐test for normally distributed variables. Associations between categorical variables were tested using χ 2 test. Linear regression models were used to evaluate the associations between n‐3 fatty acid intake (independent variable) and depression symptomatology (GDS), cognition (MoCA) and its six domains, mobility (4MWT), balance (LRMS), muscle strength (handgrip strength), diet quality (MedDietScore) and healthy aging (SAI index) (all dependent variables). Model 1 was unadjusted (crude); Model 2 was adjusted for age, sex, smoking and CVD risk factors. When GDS was the outcome Model 2 was further adjusted for social activities with friends or family and family status. Before linear regression analyses, normality of residuals was assessed using histograms and probability–probability (P–P) plots, while homoscedasticity was evaluated by scatterplots. For the GDS outcome, minor deviations were observed, but they were not considered important to significantly impact the results. No significant outliers were observed in residual plots and therefore all data were included in the analyses. Moreover, missing data were very limited (below 1.5%) and all analyses were done on complete cases. Binary logistic regression models were used to evaluate the association between n‐3 PUFA intake and the presence of long COVID‐19 symptomatology. Statistical significance was set at p‐value < 0.05, while 95% Confidence Intervals (CI) were reported for all estimates. All statistical analyses were performed using the SPSS software, version 29.0 (IBM Statistics, Greece).

sec.title: Results
sec.p: In Table 1, basic sociodemographic, anthropometric, dietary, cognitive and functional characteristics of the 449 older adult participants are presented. Overall, the mean age of the participants was 71 years; approximately 6 out of 10 were females, while 35.6% of the participants were overweight and 44.5% had obesity. Participants with low n‐3 PUFA intake (i.e., below the median value) were more likely to have smaller hip circumference (p = 0.013), worse cognition (p < 0.001), lower level of adherence to the Mediterranean diet (p < 0.001), lower SAI score (p < 0.001) and higher depression symptomatology (p < 0.001) compared to those with high n‐3 PUFA intake. No other statistically significant differences were found between the two groups (all ps > 0.05).

caption.p: Sociodemographic, anthropometrical, dietary, cognitive and functional characteristics of the total (N = 449) participants and based on the n‐3 polyunsaturated fatty acid (n‐3 PUFA) intake (low vs. high) of the participants.

fn.p: Note: Results are presented as median and interquartile ranges (skewed variables), mean and standard deviation (normally distributed variables) and frequencies (categorical variables). p values derived from Mann–Whitney U test for non‐normally distributed variables, t‐test for normally distributed ones (i.e., waist circumference) and chi‐square test for categorical values. Statistical significance was set at p‐value < 0.05, marked with bold.

Abbreviations: 4MWT, 4‐Metre Walk Test; BMI, Body Mass Index; GDS, Geriatric Depression Scale; LRMS, Laboratory for Research of the Musculoskeletal System questionnaire; MoCA, Montreal Cognitive Assessment; n, subgroup counts; N, overall total sample; SAI, Successful Aging Index.

sec.p: As residual confounding may exist in observational studies, Table 2 presents linear regression models that evaluated the association of n‐3 PUFA intake on several healthy aging indicators (i.e., MoCA, GDS, 4‐MWT LRMS, handgrip strength) among DIAPELH study participants, after considering several potential confounders. It was revealed that fish n‐3 PUFA intake was positively associated with SAI score (p < 0.001), and MoCA score (p = 0.003) but inversely with GDS score (p < 0.001) in the crude models. No statistically significant associations were observed between n‐3 PUFA intake and handgrip strength, walking speed or risk of falls in the unadjusted analyses (all ps > 0.05). The associations with SAI, MoCA and GDS remained statistically significant when potential confounders were taken into account. More specifically, for each 1 g increase in n‐3 PUFA intake, there was an approximately 0.5‐unit increase in SAI score (p < 0.001), a 0.7‐unit increase in MoCA (p = 0.012) and a 0.67‐unit decrease in GDS score (p < 0.001). When the MoCA domains were separately evaluated (Supporting Information S1: Table S1), it was found that three out of six domains (i.e., Memory, Attention and Executive) were positively associated with n‐3 PUFA intake (all ps < 0.05). Additionally, since a strong correlation between n‐3 PUFA intake and MedDietScore was observed (Spearman's rho = 0.421, p < 0.001), we examined the association of these two variables. It was found that after adjusting for age, sex, smoking habits and CVD risk factors, the level of adherence to the Mediterranean diet was positively associated with n‐3 PUFA intake (b ± SE: 1.83 ± 0.21, p < 0.001).

caption.p: Linear regression models evaluating the association of n‐3 PUFA intake (independent variable) with SAI, MoCA, GDS, handgrip strength, 4‐MWT, LRMS, MedDietScore (all dependent outcomes) among the 449 older adult Peloponnese participants.

tr.td: Model 2: Age, sex, smoking habits, CVD risk factors
Model 2: Age, sex, smoking habits, CVD risk factors, social activities with friends or family, family status
Model 2: Age, sex, smoking habits, CVD risk factors
Model 2: Age, sex, smoking habits, CVD risk factors
Model 2: Age, sex, smoking habits, CVD risk factors
Model 2: Age, sex, smoking habits, CVD risk factors

fn.p: Note: Results are presented as b‐coefficients ± standard error (SE), 95% confidence interval (95% CI) and p‐values. Statistical significance was set at p‐value < 0.05, marked with bold.

Abbreviations: 4MWT, 4‐Metre Walk Test; BMI, Body Mass Index; GDS, Geriatric Depression Scale; LRMS, Laboratory for Research of the Musculoskeletal System questionnaire; MoCA, Montreal Cognitive Assessment; n, subgroup counts; N, overall total sample; SAI, Successful Aging Index.

sec.p: A stepwise logistic regression analysis (Table 3) was conducted to assess the association between dietary n‐3 PUFA intake and the presence of long COVID symptoms, controlling for potential confounders. In the unadjusted model, higher intake of n‐3 PUFA (per mg/day) was significantly associated with lower odds of long COVID symptoms (OR = 0.434, p = 0.041). This association remained statistically significant after adjusting for age (Model 1), sex (Model 2), smoking (Model 3) and CVD risk factors (Model 4), with the fully adjusted model showing an OR of 0.412 (p = 0.036).

caption.p: Binary regression models evaluating the association of n‐3 PUFA intake (independent variable) with the presence of long COVID‐19 symptomatology (dependent variable) among the 449 older adult Peloponnesian participants.

fn.p: Note: Statistical significance was set at p‐value < 0.05, marked with bold.
Abbreviations: 95% CI, 95% confidence interval; OR, odds ratio.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: The present work evaluated the association between n‐3 PUFA intake from fish consumption and indicators of healthy aging, including the presence of long‐term COVID‐19 symptomatology, mobility and balance, mental and cognitive health, as well as the overall healthy aging through SAI index, in older Greek Peloponnesian adults. All these factors were considered indicators of healthy aging, since they reflect key dimensions of functionality and well‐being in older adults. It was revealed that n‐3 PUFA intake was associated with fewer depression symptoms, and higher adherence to the Mediterranean diet, cognition levels and levels of healthy aging in older adults. Additionally, it was found that n‐3 PUFAs are inversely correlated with the presence of long COVID‐19 symptomatology, suggesting physical vulnerability and lower quality of life and autonomy in old age. The intake of n‐3 PUFA appeared to have no association with mobility or physical performance and balance.

The findings of the present work are consistent with previous studies showing a beneficial association of n‐3 PUFA on cognitive and mental health in older adults, even though most of them investigated the impact of n‐3 PUFA through dietary supplements. More specifically, a meta‐analysis of six randomized controlled trial (RCT) studies, which included over 4500 older adults participants with a mean age of 77 years, demonstrated that n‐3 PUFA supplementation had a beneficial impact on individuals with mild to moderate depression [31]. Notably, older adults in the present study demonstrated relatively few depressive symptoms, indirectly supporting the existing literature. Accordingly, a recent review of observational studies, RCTs and meta‐analyses has reached the same conclusion, emphasizing the anti‐inflammatory properties of n‐3 PUFAs, particularly EPA and DHA, as well as their ability to stabilize neuronal membranes and enhance serotonin transmission [32]. Regarding cognitive function, although the results of the present work support a positive association between n‐3 PUFA intake and cognition, the existing literature remains conflicting [29]. Epidemiological studies generally show a favourable effect; however, some clinical trials have not confirmed significant benefits, possibly due to differences in duration, dose or population characteristics [33].

In contrast, no significant associations were observed between n‐3 PUFA intake and physical performance or balance parameters, including walking speed, handgrip strength and the risk of falling. This finding seems to be different from certain experimental studies suggesting that n‐3 PUFA intake may provide an effective, nonpharmacological approach to preserve muscle mass and function in older adults [34, 35]. However, these benefits have been documented mainly in combination with other factors [34]. Thus, the lack of association observed in the present work may reflect the need for a combined approach to produce measurable effects on the musculoskeletal system. However, when healthy aging was assessed using a composite index (i.e., SAI), without isolating individual functional components that may collectively contribute to it, a positive association was observed between n‐3 PUFA and healthy aging. This may be attributed to the complexity of the concept and the multiple factors that ultimately constitute healthy aging [5]. Similar findings were reported in the study by Lai et al., who examined 2622 older adults. Specifically, it was found that higher dietary intake of n‐3 PUFAs from fish was associated with an 18% greater likelihood of achieving healthy aging [36].

An interesting finding of this study was the negative association between n‐3 PUFAs and the presence of long COVID‐19 symptoms among Greek Peloponnesian older adults. Although we are now considered to be in the post‐COVID‐19 era, many individuals continue to be infected with the SARS‐COV‐2 and suffer for prolonged periods, especially those with comorbidities such as older adults [37]. Despite the limited number of relevant studies, a pilot, double blind RCT examined the supplementation of n‐3 PUFAs (EPA and DHA) to healthcare workers with long COVID‐19 symptoms compared to placebo. It was revealed that n‐3 PUFAs had no significant impact on the long COVID symptoms [38]. However, a review by Yang et al. on the molecular mechanisms underlying n‐3 PUFA‐mediated resistance to long COVID, concluded that oxidative stress, immune dysregulation, maladaptation of the renin‐angiotensin‐aldosterone system and coagulation system, as well as psycho‐social stress due to health, financial or social challenges, can be improved by n‐3 PUFAs and/or their biologically active metabolites [18].

As revealed in the present study, older adults who consume higher quantities of n‐3 PUFAs were more likely to have higher adherence to the Mediterranean diet compared to their counterparts with lower n‐3 PUFA intake. This finding suggests that n‐3 PUFA intake may be associated with healthier dietary profiles. Actually, humans cannot internally synthesize n‐3 PUFAs due to the absence of specialized enzymes [39]. Therefore, n‐3 PUFAs are essential nutrients that must be obtained from dietary sources [39]. The main source of these nutrients is fish, which are widely available and traditionally consumed in Mediterranean countries, including Greece. The dietary habits and the environment may partly explain the observed association between n‐3 PUFA intake and adherence to the Mediterranean diet.

To the best of our knowledge, this is one of the first studies evaluating the association between n‐3 PUFA intake via fish of Greek Peloponnesians and healthy aging indicators. However, the findings of the present study should be evaluated considering the existing limitations. N‐3 PUFA intake was estimated using an FFQ without biochemical validation (e.g., plasma or erythrocyte fatty acid levels). Nevertheless, in epidemiological studies, FFQs are widely used for dietary assessment and may provide valuable information. Additionally, due to its observational nature, causal associations are not allowed, while social desirability and recall bias may also exist in self‐reported questionnaires. The SAI index employed in this study may not accurately evaluate healthy aging, since there are many approaches for its definition. Nevertheless, it is a validated tool and used in other aging‐related studies [30, 40, 41]. In parallel, the design of the study cannot exclude the possible effects of other constituents found in fish, such as selenium, polar lipids, vitamin D, zinc which may confound our findings.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: To conclude, the findings of this study indicate that a higher consumption of n‐3 PUFA from fish is favourably associated with several indicators of healthy aging, including cognitive and mental health, Successful Aging Index, an index that encompasses several indicators of healthy aging as well as long COVID‐19 symptomatology. The results underscore the significance of nutrition in older adults, specifically highlighting a potential association between dietary n‐3 PUFA and functionality maintenance. Future prospective studies may validate these associations and contribute to the development of targeted nutritional strategies for older adults.

sec.title: Author Contributions
sec.p: Aristea Gioxari and Alexandra Foscolou: conceptualization. Aristea Gioxari, Alexandra Foscolou and Trisevgeni Pratti: methodology. Aristea Gioxari, Paraskevi Detopoulou, Konstantina Argyri, Georgios I. Panoutsopoulos and Sotirios Vamvakas: validation. Alexandra Foscolou: formal analysis. Trisevgeni Pratti, Giannoula Nikolaou, Antigone Kouskouti, Anthoula Malikourti, Maria Petrogianni, Ermelinda Shurdha and Eftychia Psaroudaki: investigation. Alexandra Foscolou, Trisevgeni Pratti, Giannoula Nikolaou, Anthoula Malikourti, Maria Petrogianni, Ermelinda Shurdha and Eftychia Psaroudaki: data curation. Alexandra Foscolou: writing – original draft preparation. Aristea Gioxari, Alexandra Foscolou, Paraskevi Detopoulou, Konstantina Argyri, Georgios I. Panoutsopoulos and Sotirios Vamvakas: writing – review and editing. Aristea Gioxari: supervision. All authors have read and agreed to the published version of the manuscript.

sec.title: Ethics Statement
sec.p: The present study was approved by the School of Health Sciences Research Ethics Committee at the University of Peloponnese (Approval no 04/11‐09‐2023). The study was conducted in accordance with the principles of the Declaration of Helsinki. Participants were apprised of the study's objectives and methodologies, and all provided signed informed consent before enrolment.

sec.title: Conflicts of Interest
sec.p: The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

caption.p: Supplementary Table 1: Linear regression models evaluating the association of n‐3 PUFA intake (independent variable) with Memory, Language, Attention, Executive, Visuospatial and Orientation MoCA domains (all dependent outcomes) among the 449 older adult Peloponnese participants.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: The authors are particularly thankful to all participants in the DIAPELH study, as well as to all investigators. The publication of this article in OA mode was financially supported by HEAL‐Link.

sec.title: Data Availability Statement
sec.p: The data presented in this study are available on request from the corresponding author due to privacy and ethical reasons.
